rules.description: Use active voice. Avoid passive constructions where the subject receives the action.

bad: The account was logged into by Marti.

rules.description: Write plainly. Value clarity above all and avoid fluffy metaphors or cheap plays to emotion.
Avoid slang and corporate jargon. Write in plain English rather than technical B2B terminology.
Use positive phrasing rather than negative constructions. Tell people what they can do, not what they can't.
Keep humor straight-faced, subtle, and a touch eccentric. Avoid forced jokes.
Be genuine and relatable. Speak in a familiar, warm, and accessible way rather than corporate-sounding.
Don't be condescending. Bring the reader in on the joke rather than excluding them.
Prioritize clarity over entertainment. It's always more important to be clear than entertaining.
Use contractions freely (it's, don't, we're) for an informal, friendly tone.
Avoid the word "automagical" — it's corporate jargon.

bad: Our automagical workflow takes care of it.

rules.description: Avoid "leverage" as a verb. Use "use" instead.

good: Use these tools to grow your audience.
bad: Leverage these tools to grow your audience.

rules.description: Avoid "incentivize" — prefer plainer alternatives like "encourage" or "reward."
Avoid buzzwords like "disruption" and "thought leader."
Avoid "learnings." Use "lessons" or "what we learned."
Avoid corporate marketing jargon like "funnel" when plainer language works.
Avoid hyperbolic role descriptors: ninja, rockstar, wizard, unicorn (unless literal).
Avoid "crushing it" and "killing it."
Avoid "crazy" and "insane," especially when describing people.
Avoid "best-in-breed" and similar empty superlatives.
Avoid "blacklist" and "whitelist." Use "blocklist" and "allowlist" instead.
Avoid "grandfathered." Use "legacy" or "existing users" instead.
Write "email" as one word, no hyphen, lowercase unless at the start of a sentence.

good: Send the email to your subscribers.
bad: Send the e-mail to your subscribers.

rules.description: Don't capitalize "internet," "online," or "website" mid-sentence.
Write "e-commerce" with a hyphen.
"Login" is a noun/adjective; "log in" (two words) is the verb. Same pattern for signup/sign up, setup/set up.
"Signup" is a noun/adjective; "sign up" (two words) is the verb.
Use "drop-down" (hyphenated) as a noun or adjective; "drop down" (no hyphen) only as a verb.
Use "pop-up" (hyphenated) as a noun or adjective; "pop up" only as a verb.
Write "OK" in all caps, no periods. Not "O.K." or "okay."
Write "website," "homepage," "username," "checkbox," "coworker," "hashtag," and "nonprofit" as one word, no hyphen.
Write "Mailchimp" with capital M and lowercase c. Refer to the company as "we," not "it."
Use the Oxford (serial) comma in lists of three or more items.

good: David admires his parents, Oprah, and Justin Timberlake.
bad: David admires his parents, Oprah and Justin Timberlake.

rules.description: Use exclamation points sparingly, and never more than one at a time. Avoid them in failure messages and alerts.
Use title case for headers (capitalize all words except articles, prepositions, and conjunctions). Use sentence case for body copy.
Spell out numbers when they start a sentence; otherwise use numerals (including ordinals).
Add commas to numbers with more than three digits.
Use the % symbol rather than spelling out "percent."
Don't use ampersands in place of "and," except in official brand or company names.
Use a true em dash (—) without spaces around it to offset asides. Don't use double hyphens.

good: The plan—if you can call it that—worked.
bad: The plan -- if you can call it that -- worked.

rules.description: Minimize semicolons. Prefer an em dash or a new sentence.
Use "they," "them," and "their" as singular pronouns when gender is unknown or non-specified.

good: When a customer signs up, send them a welcome email.

rules.description: Use one space between sentences, never two.
Left-align body text. Never center- or right-align long-form copy.
Don't use underline formatting (it implies a hyperlink). Don't combine italic, bold, all-caps, and underline.
Write times as numerals with a space before lowercase am/pm. Omit minutes on the hour. Use an en dash for ranges.
Spell out abbreviations and acronyms on first mention, then use the short form. Exception: well-known ones like API or HTML.
Link text should describe the destination or action. Avoid "click here" and "learn more."
Include alt text on all meaningful images. For charts and graphs, describe the data so readers don't need to see the image.
Write short sentences and use familiar words. Avoid jargon and slang.
Headings must be nested and consecutive (H1 → H2 → H3). Never skip a heading level for styling.
Lead with the most important information. Group related topics in the same paragraph; separate different topics with headings.
Avoid directional instructions that require seeing the layout ("the sidebar on the right," "the button below").

bad: Select from the options in the right sidebar

rules.description: Avoid disability-based idioms like "lame," "crazy," "insane," or "falling on deaf ears."
Avoid "guys" for mixed groups and "girls" for women. Use neutral terms.
Use gender-neutral role names: "server" not "waitress," "businessperson" not "businessman," "firefighter" not "fireman."
Don't describe people as "young," "old," or "elderly." Mention age only when relevant, and give the specific number.

good: The CEO, 16, just got her driver's license.
bad: The young CEO just got her driver's license.

rules.description: When writing about disability, avoid "suffer," "victim," and "handicapped" (except in fixed terms like "handicapped parking").
Capitalize racial and ethnic group names (Black, Asian American, Latino). Keep "white" lowercase.
